NKJV.1: Now concerning things offered to idols: We know that we all have knowledge. Knowledge puffs up, but love edifies.
NKJV.2: And if anyone thinks that he knows anything, he knows nothing yet as he ought to know.
NKJV.3: But if anyone loves God, this one is known by Him.
NKJV.4: Therefore concerning the eating of things offered to idols, we know that an idol is nothing in the world, and that there is no other God but one.
NKJV.5: For even if there are so-called gods, whether in heaven or on earth (as there are many gods and many lords),
NKJV.6: yet for us there is one God, the Father, of whom are all things, and we for Him; and one Lord Jesus Christ, through whom are all things, and through whom we live.
NKJV.7: However, there is not in everyone that knowledge; for some, with consciousness of the idol, until now eat it as a thing offered to an idol; and their conscience, being weak, is defiled.
NKJV.8: But food does not commend us to God; for neither if we eat are we the better, nor if we do not eat are we the worse.
NKJV.9: But beware lest somehow this liberty of yours become a stumbling block to those who are weak.
NKJV.10: For if anyone sees you who have knowledge eating in an idol’s temple, will not the conscience of him who is weak be emboldened to eat those things offered to idols?
NKJV.11: And because of your knowledge shall the weak brother perish, for whom Christ died?
NKJV.12: But when you thus sin against the brethren, and wound their weak conscience, you sin against Christ.
NKJV.13: Therefore, if food makes my brother stumble, I will never again eat meat, lest I make my brother stumble.

ESV.1: Now concerning  food offered to idols: we know that “all of us possess knowledge.” This “knowledge” puffs up, but love builds up.
ESV.2: If anyone imagines that he knows something, he does not yet know as he ought to know.
ESV.3: But if anyone loves God, he is known by God.
ESV.4: Therefore, as to the eating of food offered to idols, we know that “an idol has no real existence,” and that “there is no God but one.”
ESV.5: For although there may be so-called gods in heaven or on earth — as indeed there are many “gods” and many “lords” —
ESV.6: yet for us there is one God, the Father, from whom are all things and for whom we exist, and one Lord, Jesus Christ, through whom are all things and through whom we exist.
ESV.7: However, not all possess this knowledge. But some, through former association with idols, eat food as really offered to an idol, and their conscience, being weak, is defiled.
ESV.8: Food will not commend us to God. We are no worse off if we do not eat, and no better off if we do.
ESV.9: But take care that this right of yours does not somehow become a stumbling block to the weak.
ESV.10: For if anyone sees you who have knowledge eating  in an idol's temple, will he not be encouraged,  if his conscience is weak, to eat food offered to idols?
ESV.11: And so by your knowledge this weak person is destroyed, the brother for whom Christ died.
ESV.12: Thus, sinning against your brothers  and wounding their conscience when it is weak, you sin against Christ.
ESV.13: Therefore, if food makes my brother stumble, I will never eat meat, lest I make my brother stumble.

NASB.1: Now concerning things sacrificed to idols, we know that we all have knowledge. Knowledge makes arrogant, but love edifies.
NASB.2: If anyone supposes that he knows anything, he has not yet known as he ought to know;
NASB.3: but if anyone loves God, he is known by Him.
NASB.4: Therefore concerning the eating of things sacrificed to idols, we know that there is no such thing as an idol in the world, and that there is no God but one.
NASB.5: For even if there are so-called gods whether in heaven or on earth, as indeed there are many gods and many lords,
NASB.6: yet for us there is [but] one God, the Father, from whom are all things and we [exist] for Him; and one Lord, Jesus Christ, by whom are all things, and we [exist] through Him.
NASB.7: However not all men have this knowledge; but some, being accustomed to the idol until now, eat [food] as if it were sacrificed to an idol; and their conscience being weak is defiled.
NASB.8: But food will not commend us to God; we are neither the worse if we do not eat, nor the better if we do eat.
NASB.9: But take care that this liberty of yours does not somehow become a stumbling block to the weak.
NASB.10: For if someone sees you, who have knowledge, dining in an idol's temple, will not his conscience, if he is weak, be strengthened to eat things sacrificed to idols?
NASB.11: For through your knowledge he who is weak is ruined, the brother for whose sake Christ died.
NASB.12: And so, by sinning against the brethren and wounding their conscience when it is weak, you sin against Christ.
NASB.13: Therefore, if food causes my brother to stumble, I will never eat meat again, so that I will not cause my brother to stumble.

NLT.1: Now regarding your question about food that has been offered to idols. Yes, we know that “we all have knowledge” about this issue. But while knowledge makes us feel important, it is love that strengthens the church.
NLT.2: Anyone who claims to know all the answers doesn’t really know very much.
NLT.3: But the person who loves God is the one whom God recognizes.
NLT.4: So, what about eating meat that has been offered to idols? Well, we all know that an idol is not really a god and that there is only one God.
NLT.5: There may be so-called gods both in heaven and on earth, and some people actually worship many gods and many lords.
NLT.6:
But for us, There is one God, the Father,
by whom all things were created,
and for whom we live.
And there is one Lord, Jesus Christ,
through whom all things were created,
and through whom we live.
NLT.7: However, not all believers know this. Some are accustomed to thinking of idols as being real, so when they eat food that has been offered to idols, they think of it as the worship of real gods, and their weak consciences are violated.
NLT.8: It’s true that we can’t win God’s approval by what we eat. We don’t lose anything if we don’t eat it, and we don’t gain anything if we do.
NLT.9: But you must be careful so that your freedom does not cause others with a weaker conscience to stumble.
NLT.10: For if others see you — with your “superior knowledge” — eating in the temple of an idol, won’t they be encouraged to violate their conscience by eating food that has been offered to an idol?
NLT.11: So because of your superior knowledge, a weak believer for whom Christ died will be destroyed.
NLT.12: And when you sin against other believers by encouraging them to do something they believe is wrong, you are sinning against Christ.
NLT.13: So if what I eat causes another believer to sin, I will never eat meat again as long as I live — for I don’t want to cause another believer to stumble.

WEB.1: Now concerning things sacrificed to idols: We know that we all have knowledge. Knowledge puffs up, but love builds up.
WEB.2: But if anyone thinks that he knows anything, he doesn’t yet know as he ought to know.
WEB.3: But if anyone loves God, the same is known by him.
WEB.4: Therefore concerning the eating of things sacrificed to idols, we know that no idol is anything in the world, and that there is no other God but one.
WEB.5: For though there are things that are called “gods”, whether in the heavens or on earth; as there are many “gods” and many “lords”;
WEB.6: yet to us there is one God, the Father, of whom are all things, and we for him; and one Lord, Jesus Christ, through whom are all things, and we live through him.
WEB.7: However, that knowledge isn’t in all men. But some, with consciousness of the idol until now, eat as of a thing sacrificed to an idol, and their conscience, being weak, is defiled.
WEB.8: But food will not commend us to God. For neither, if we don’t eat, are we the worse; nor, if we eat, are we the better.
WEB.9: But be careful that by no means does this liberty of yours become a stumbling block to the weak.
WEB.10: For if a man sees you who have knowledge sitting in an idol’s temple, won’t his conscience, if he is weak, be emboldened to eat things sacrificed to idols?
WEB.11: And through your knowledge, he who is weak perishes, the brother for whose sake Christ died.
WEB.12: Thus, sinning against the brothers, and wounding their conscience when it is weak, you sin against Christ.
WEB.13: Therefore if food causes my brother to stumble, I will eat no meat forever more, that I don’t cause my brother to stumble.

CSB17.1: Now about food sacrificed to idols: <sup>ⓝ</sup> We know that “we all have knowledge.” Knowledge puffs up, <sup>ⓐ</sup> but love <sup>ⓑ</sup> builds up.
CSB17.2: If anyone thinks he knows anything, he does not yet know it as he ought to know it. <sup>ⓒ</sup>
CSB17.3: But if anyone loves God, <sup>ⓓ</sup> he is known <sup>ⓔ</sup> by him.
CSB17.4: About eating food sacrificed to idols, then, we know that “an idol is nothing in the world,” <sup>[1]</sup> and that “there is no God but one.” <sup>ⓕ</sup>
CSB17.5: For even if there are so-called gods, <sup>ⓖ</sup> whether in heaven or on earth — as there are many “gods” and many “lords” —
CSB17.6: yet for us there is one God, the Father. <sup>ⓗ</sup> All things are from him, <sup>ⓘ</sup> and we exist for him. And there is one Lord, Jesus Christ. All things are through him, and we exist through him. <sup>ⓙ</sup>
CSB17.7: However, not everyone has this knowledge. Some have been so used to idolatry up until now that when they eat food sacrificed to an idol, their conscience, being weak, is defiled.
CSB17.8: Food will not bring us close to God. <sup>[2]</sup> We are not worse off if we don’t eat, and we are not better if we do eat. <sup>ⓚ</sup>
CSB17.9: But be careful that this right of yours in no way becomes a stumbling block <sup>ⓛ</sup> to the weak.
CSB17.10: For if someone sees you, the one who has knowledge, dining in an idol’s temple, won’t his weak conscience be encouraged <sup>[3]</sup> to eat food offered to idols?
CSB17.11: So the weak person, the brother or sister for whom Christ died, <sup>ⓜ</sup> is ruined <sup>[4]</sup> <sup>ⓝ</sup> by your knowledge. <sup>ⓐ</sup>
CSB17.12: Now when you sin like this against brothers and sisters and wound their weak conscience, you are sinning against Christ.
CSB17.13: Therefore, if food causes my brother or sister to fall, I will never again eat meat, <sup>ⓑ</sup> so that I won’t cause my brother or sister to fall.

AMP.1: Now about food sacrificed to idols, we know that we all have knowledge [concerning this]. Knowledge [alone] makes [people self-righteously] arrogant, but love [that unselfishly seeks the best for others] builds up and encourages others to grow [in wisdom].
AMP.2: If anyone imagines that he knows and understands anything [of divine matters, without love], he has not yet known as he ought to know.
AMP.3: But if anyone loves God [with awe-filled reverence, obedience and gratitude], he is known by Him [as His very own and is greatly loved].
AMP.4: In this matter, then, of eating food offered to idols, we know that an idol is nothing in the world [it has no real existence], and that there is no God but one.
AMP.5: For even if there are so-called gods, whether in heaven or on earth, as indeed there are many gods and many lords,
AMP.6: yet for us there is but one God, the Father, who is the source of all things, and we exist for Him; and one Lord, Jesus Christ, by whom are all things [that have been created], and we [believers exist and have life and have been redeemed] through Him.
AMP.7: However, not all [believers] have this knowledge. But some, being accustomed [throughout their lives] to [thinking of] the idol until now [as real and living], still eat food as if it were sacrificed to an idol; and because their conscience is weak, it is defiled (guilty, ashamed).
AMP.8: Now food will not commend us to God nor bring us close to Him; we are no worse off if we do not eat, nor are we better if we do eat.
AMP.9: Only be careful that this liberty of yours [this power to choose] does not somehow become a stumbling block [that is, a temptation to sin] to the weak [in conscience].
AMP.10: For if someone sees you, a person having knowledge, eating in an idol's temple, then if he is weak, will he not be encouraged to eat things sacrificed to idols [and violate his own convictions]?
AMP.11: For through your knowledge (spiritual maturity) this weak man is ruined [that is, he suffers in his spiritual life], the brother for whom Christ died.
AMP.12: And when you sin against the brothers and sisters in this way and wound their weak conscience [by confusing them], you sin against Christ.
AMP.13: Therefore, if [my eating a certain] food causes my brother to stumble (sin), I will not eat [such] meat ever again, so that I will not cause my brother to stumble.

MSG.1: The question keeps coming up regarding meat that has been offered up to an idol: Should you attend meals where such meat is served, or not? We sometimes tend to think we know all we need to know to answer these kinds of questions—
MSG.2: but sometimes our humble hearts can help us more than our proud minds.
MSG.3: We never really know enough until we recognize that God alone knows it all.
MSG.4: Some people say, quite rightly, that idols have no actual existence, that there's nothing to them, that there is no God other than our one God,
MSG.5: that no matter how many of these so-called gods are named and worshiped they still don't add up to anything but a tall story.
MSG.6: They say—again, quite rightly—that there is only one God the Father, that everything comes from him, and that he wants us to live for him. Also, they say that there is only one Master—Jesus the Messiah—and that everything is for his sake, including us. Yes. It's true.
MSG.7: In strict logic, then, nothing happened to the meat when it was offered up to an idol. It's just like any other meat. I know that, and you know that. But knowing isn't everything. If it becomes everything, some people end up as know-it-alls who treat others as know-nothings. Real knowledge isn't that insensitive. We need to be sensitive to the fact that we're not all at the same level of understanding in this. Some of you have spent your entire lives eating "idol meat," and are sure that there's something bad in the meat that then becomes something bad inside of you. An imagination and conscience shaped under those conditions isn't going to change overnight.
MSG.8: But fortunately God doesn't grade us on our diet. We're neither commended when we clean our plate nor reprimanded when we just can't stomach it.
MSG.9: But God does care when you use your freedom carelessly in a way that leads a Christian still vulnerable to those old associations to be thrown off track.
MSG.10: For instance, say you flaunt your freedom by going to a banquet thrown in honor of idols, where the main course is meat sacrificed to idols. Isn't there great danger if someone still struggling over this issue, someone who looks up to you as knowledgeable and mature, sees you go into that banquet? The danger is that he will become terribly confused—maybe even to the point of getting mixed up himself in what his conscience tells him is wrong.
MSG.11: Christ gave up his life for that person. Wouldn't you at least be willing to give up going to dinner for him—because, as you say, it doesn't really make any difference? But it does make a difference if you hurt your friend terribly, risking his eternal ruin!
MSG.12: When you hurt your friend, you hurt Christ. A free meal here and there isn't worth it at the cost of even one of these "weak ones."
MSG.13: So, never go to these idol-tainted meals if there's any chance it will trip up one of your brothers or sisters.

NIV.1:
Food Sacrificed to Idols
Now about food sacrificed to idols: We know that we all possess knowledge. Knowledge puffs up, but love builds up.
NIV.2: The man who thinks he knows something does not yet know as he ought to know.
NIV.3: But the man who loves God is known by God.
NIV.4: So then, about eating food sacrificed to idols: We know that an idol is nothing at all in the world and that there is no God but one.
NIV.5: For even if there are so-called gods, whether in heaven or on earth (as indeed there are many “gods” and many “lords”),
NIV.6: yet for us there is but one God, the Father, from whom all things came and for whom we live; and there is but one Lord, Jesus Christ, through whom all things came and through whom we live.
NIV.7: But not everyone knows this. Some people are still so accustomed to idols that when they eat such food they think of it as having been sacrificed to an idol, and since their conscience is weak, it is defiled.
NIV.8: But food does not bring us near to God; we are no worse if we do not eat, and no better if we do.
NIV.9: Be careful, however, that the exercise of your freedom does not become a stumbling block to the weak.
NIV.10: For if anyone with a weak conscience sees you who have this knowledge eating in an idol’s temple, won’t he be emboldened to eat what has been sacrificed to idols?
NIV.11: So this weak brother, for whom Christ died, is destroyed by your knowledge.
NIV.12: When you sin against your brothers in this way and wound their weak conscience, you sin against Christ.
NIV.13: Therefore, if what I eat causes my brother to fall into sin, I will never eat meat again, so that I will not cause him to fall.

NRSVCE.1: Now concerning food sacrificed to idols: we know that “all of us possess knowledge.” Knowledge puffs up, but love builds up.
NRSVCE.2: Anyone who claims to know something does not yet have the necessary knowledge;
NRSVCE.3: but anyone who loves God is known by him.
NRSVCE.4: Hence, as to the eating of food offered to idols, we know that “no idol in the world really exists,” and that “there is no God but one.”
NRSVCE.5: Indeed, even though there may be so-called gods in heaven or on earth — as in fact there are many gods and many lords —
NRSVCE.6: yet for us there is one God, the Father, from whom are all things and for whom we exist, and one Lord, Jesus Christ, through whom are all things and through whom we exist.
NRSVCE.7: It is not everyone, however, who has this knowledge. Since some have become so accustomed to idols until now, they still think of the food they eat as food offered to an idol; and their conscience, being weak, is defiled.
NRSVCE.8: “Food will not bring us close to God.” We are no worse off if we do not eat, and no better off if we do.
NRSVCE.9: But take care that this liberty of yours does not somehow become a stumbling block to the weak.
NRSVCE.10: For if others see you, who possess knowledge, eating in the temple of an idol, might they not, since their conscience is weak, be encouraged to the point of eating food sacrificed to idols?
NRSVCE.11: So by your knowledge those weak believers for whom Christ died are destroyed.
NRSVCE.12: But when you thus sin against members of your family, and wound their conscience when it is weak, you sin against Christ.
NRSVCE.13: Therefore, if food is a cause of their falling, I will never eat meat, so that I may not cause one of them to fall.

NET.1: With regard to food sacrificed to idols, we know that “we all have knowledge.” Knowledge puffs up, but love builds up.
NET.2: If someone thinks he knows something, he does not yet know to the degree that he needs to know.
NET.3: But if someone loves God, he is known by God.
NET.4: With regard then to eating food sacrificed to idols, we know that “an idol in this world is nothing,” and that “there is no God but one.”
NET.5: If after all there are so-called gods, whether in heaven or on earth (as there are many gods and many lords),
NET.6: yet for us there is one God, the Father, from whom are all things and for whom we live, and one Lord, Jesus Christ, through whom are all things and through whom we live.
NET.7: But this knowledge is not shared by all. And some, by being accustomed to idols in former times, eat this food as an idol sacrifice, and their conscience, because it is weak, is defiled.
NET.8: Now food will not bring us close to God. We are no worse if we do not eat and no better if we do.
NET.9: But be careful that this liberty of yours does not become a hindrance to the weak.
NET.10: For if someone weak sees you who possess knowledge dining in an idol’s temple, will not his conscience be “strengthened” to eat food offered to idols?
NET.11: So by your knowledge the weak brother or sister, for whom Christ died, is destroyed.
NET.12: If you sin against your brothers or sisters in this way and wound their weak conscience, you sin against Christ.
NET.13: For this reason, if food causes my brother or sister to sin, I will never eat meat again, so that I may not cause one of them to sin.
